steps: If \(n\) spaces fill a 135-foot curb, each space has width \(\frac{135}{n}\) feet. The standard width constraint gives \(7.5 \le \frac{135}{n} \le 9.0\).
From \(\frac{135}{n} \le 9\): multiply both sides by \(n\) to get \(135 \le 9n\), so \(n \ge 15\).
From \(7.5 \le \frac{135}{n}\): multiply both sides by \(n\) to get \(7.5n \le 135\), so \(n \le 18\).
eliminationHtml:
• Choice A — '\(18 \le n \le 135\)': The upper bound 135 has no basis; a 135-foot curb cannot hold 135 spaces each at least 7.5 feet wide. ✗
  • Choice B — '\(7.5 \le n \le 9\)': These are the widths in feet, not the number of spaces. ✗
  • Choice C — '\(15 \le n \le 135\)': The lower bound 15 is correct, but the upper bound 135 is far too high. ✗
  • Choice D — '\(15 \le n \le 18\)': \(135 \div 9 = 15\) (minimum spaces) and \(135 \div 7.5 = 18\) (maximum spaces). Both bounds are correct. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
